title: Bound-state-protected phase metrology for a quantum emitter in a Su-Schrieffer-Heeger bath
abstract: We study local phase estimation for a single quantum emitter coupled to a bosonic Su-Schrieffer-Heeger (SSH) bath within a microscopic lattice model. Dimerization opens a central gap supporting an in-gap emitter-bath bound state, which suppresses complete relaxation of the emitter coherence. A Dyson-equation analysis yields the local bath Green's function, the in-gap bound-state condition, and the emitter residue controlling the retained phase information. The phase quantum Fisher information links gap formation, detuning, and emitter-bath coupling to the post-transient metrological response. At resonance, stronger coupling enhances transient hybridization but reduces the retained signal by lowering the emitter weight in the bound state. Away from resonance, late-time averages, retention times, and useful interrogation windows track how phase-information protection weakens as the emitter is tuned toward and beyond the band edge. A uniform-chain control shows that the retained signal disappears when the gap closes. In the bulk local-coupling geometry considered here, the response is insensitive to the sign of the dimerization, so the protocol probes spectral-gap physics and bound-state support rather than the SSH winding sector.
summary: This paper analyzes phase metrology for a quantum emitter coupled to a dimerized SSH lattice bath. It shows that the ability to retain phase information is protected by a spectral gap, which supports an in-gap bound state. The metrological signal strength is quantified by the QFI, which tracks the spectral gap physics, providing a robust diagnostic tool for engineered quantum systems.
main_problem: The study investigates how the structured environment provided by a Su-Schrieffer-Heeger (SSH) bath protects and governs the persistence of phase information (metrology) in a quantum emitter.
main_result: The late-time phase-Quantum Fisher Information (QFI) is directly protected by the existence of an in-gap bound state, and this protection degrades predictably as the emitter detuning moves away from the gap center or as coupling strength increases.
method: The analysis employs a microscopic approach using Dyson-equation analysis to derive the local bath Green's function, allowing for the calculation of the phase-QFI in the single-excitation sector.
model_or_system: The system consists of a single quantum emitter (two-level system) coupled locally to a bosonic bath modeled by a dimerized Su-Schrieffer-Heeger (SSH) lattice Hamiltonian.
key_observables: Quantum Fisher Information (QFI), the emitter bound-state residue ($Z_{BS}$), and the late-time averaged phase-QFI ($\overline{F(\phi)}_{Q}$).
important_parameters: Dimerization parameter ($d$), emitter detuning ($\Delta$), and coupling strength ($g$).
main_assumptions: The analysis is restricted to the bulk, local-coupling geometry, meaning it probes spectral-gap physics rather than the SSH winding sector.
figures_summary: Figures illustrate the SSH dispersion relation showing the central gap, and plot the QFI vs. dimerization strength, coupling strength, and detuning, demonstrating the gap-protected nature of the signal.
paper_structure: The paper builds from defining the SSH model and the metrological observable (QFI) to deriving the bound-state condition via Dyson equations. It then systematically analyzes the QFI dependence on dimerization, coupling strength, and detuning, culminating in a detailed characterization of the gap-edge crossover.
why_interesting: This work provides a concrete, lattice-based realization of how topological spectral features (the gap) can be harnessed to engineer robust quantum sensing protocols, linking condensed matter topology directly to quantum information metrics.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1. Model overview. (a) Single quantum emitter locally coupled with strength g to a photonic structure that leads to a sublattice of a dimerized bosonic SSH chain with intracell and intercell hoppings J1 = J(1 + d) and J2 = J(1 −d). (b) SSH dispersion for a representative dimerization, shown together with the central band gap 4J|d|.

FIG. 2. Time-dependent phase-QFI F (ϕ) Q (t) at ∆= 0 and g = 0.4J for four representative values of the dimerization parameter, d = 0.7, 0.5, 0.3, and 0.1. The horizontal lines in- dicate the analytic central in-gap bound-state prediction de- rived below [see Eq. (23)]. Numerical results are obtained for a finite SSH chain with 2L + 1 unit cells, L = 500, using a Krylov subspace of dimension 350.

FIG. 4. Time-dependent phase-QFI F (ϕ) Q (t) at zero detuning, ∆= 0, and fixed dimerization d = 0.3 for four representative emitter-bath coupling strengths, g/J = 0.1, 0.4, 0.6, and 0.8. The horizontal lines indicate the corresponding central in-gap bound-state predictions from Eq. (23). Numerical results are obtained for a finite SSH chain with 2L+1 unit cells, L = 500, using a Krylov subspace of dimension 350.

FIG. 3. Comparison between numerical and analytic bound- state diagnostics at ∆= 0 and g = 0.4J. (a) Emitter bound- state residue ZBS extracted from the in-gap eigenstate of a finite SSH chain, together with the analytic expression in Eq. (25). (b) Retained phase-QFI obtained from the cen- tral in-gap bound-state relation F (ϕ) Q = Z2 BS, together with the analytic result in Eq. (23). Numerical diagonalizations use a finite chain with 2L + 1 unit cells and L = 220.

FIG. 5. Time-dependent phase-QFI F (ϕ) Q (t) in the uniform- chain limit d = 0 at ∆= 0 and g = 0.4J. Numerical results are obtained for a finite SSH chain with 2L + 1 unit cells, L = 500, using a Krylov-space propagation of dimension 350.
